terms.definition: The standardised TOML file at a Python project's root that declares the build system (`[build-system]`), project metadata (`[project]`), and per-tool configuration (`[tool.*]`).
The library invoked by pip/build to convert source into a wheel/sdist (also called a packaging backend). Examples: setuptools, hatchling, flit-core, poetry-core, pdm-backend.
The tool that drives a build by calling the backend through the PEP 517 interface (also called a packaging frontend). Examples: pip, `python -m build`, uv, hatch.
Python's pre-built distribution format (file extension `.whl`, often called a binary distribution). Installable without running setup code; the modern default Python install format.
A source distribution — a packaged source tree plus metadata, shipped as a `.tar.gz`. pip can build it into a wheel on the target machine when no matching wheel is available.
The PEP that defines the build-backend interface — how frontends like pip invoke backends like setuptools or hatchling to produce wheels.
The PEP that defines `[build-system]` in `pyproject.toml` — the way to declare a project's build requirements without a `setup.py`.
The PEP that standardises the `[project]` metadata table (name, version, dependencies, scripts, ...) so any build backend can read the same project info.
The PEP that defines how editable installs (`pip install -e .`) work via the build backend, replacing the legacy `setup.py develop` mechanism.
A named hook installed by pip that points to a Python callable. The most common case is CLI commands, declared under `[project.scripts]` and surfaced as wrapper scripts on PATH.
Named groups of extra dependencies (also called extras) declared in `[project.optional-dependencies]`; installed via `pip install pkg[name]`, e.g. `pip install ragapp[dev]`.
